feedback_entries.highlighted_text: While this is true in the present, ground-up initiatives and governments are still trying to reduce reliance on deforestation and provide alternative methods of utilising their forests without deforestation. This is because there has been growing awareness of climate change and the need for sustainable development that many governments, including that of LDCs, have been trying to commit to. For example, the REDD+ project in Cambodia was successful in protecting 70% of the Seima Protection Forest due to joint efforts by the indigenous Ponnong people, the Cambodian Forestry Association, and the Wildlife Conservation Society in Cambodia. Through this partnership, a green credits system and ecotourism was set up, encouraging locals to transition from logging to more sustainable forms of employment.
feedback_entries.AI_feedback: You've done well in presenting a balanced evaluation by acknowledging efforts to reduce deforestation despite economic challenges, and your REDD+ Cambodia example effectively illustrates how ground-up initiatives can lead to positive outcomes. feedback_entries.highlighted_text: Countries may be able to stop deforestation if they implement policies to counter deforestation. These can be done through policies such as the creation of protected areas. Protected areas are land which has legislation that prevent to deforestation from occurring. This is done via patrol officers who ensure that illegal logging door not occur as well as the creation of nature reserves that are highly watched and regulated. In Brazil, 1/3 of the land in protected areas with the highest level of control possible. Between 2000 and 2001, Brazil was able to create 487000km² of protected areas. This was successful in decreasing deforestation as the rates of deforestation fell by 38% from 2004- 2006.
